subject: Statistics
title: The Ecological Fallacy
description: A short lesson to help you apply The Ecological Fallacy.

hook.text: Countries with more chocolate consumption win more Nobel Prizes. Should you eat chocolate to get smarter?

buildup.text: The ecological fallacy assumes that patterns in groups apply to individuals within those groups.

discovery.text: Wealthy countries buy more chocolate and fund more research. The link is wealth, not cocoa improving brains.

twist.text: Entire research fields have been misled by ecological correlations. Regional data can't prove individual causation.

climax.text: What's true for the average of a group isn't necessarily true for any member of that group.

punchline.text: Groups and individuals follow different rules. Don't confuse the two.

quiz.question: What is the ecological fallacy?
options: Assuming that patterns observed in groups apply to individual members of those groups
Making predictions about ecology from economic data
Using too-small sample sizes in environmental studies
